raw.title: embedded-mongo
raw.text:
== What ==

A Ruby implementation of the MongoDB interface.

Just swap out the line "Mongo::Connection.new" in your application
with "EmbeddedMongo::Connection.new" and leave the rest of the code
alone.  No server process needed!

E.g.

    conn = EmbeddedMongo::Connection.new
    coll = conn['test']['embedded']
    coll.insert({ 'hello' => 'world' })
    coll.find.to_a
    # => [{"_id"=>BSON::ObjectId('4e1b4027f626214e9e000001'), "hello"=>"world"}]

== Goal ==

embedded-mongo's goal is to provide the same interface as mongodb but
be embedded inside the calling process.  This allows unit tests to be
run without the overhead of database roundtrips or the creation of
ad-hoc mock layers.  It also allows one to start using the mongodb
interface for new projects without having to set up a real database.
I don't think there's a use-case for it in production, though I could
be wrong.

== Status ==

Most of the query semantics have been coded.  Many of the database
management operations have not.  Nothing involving execution of
JavaScript has been coded, because that sounds like work.  Persistence
hasn't been added, though that could be as easy as a Marshal or YAML
dump when needed.

I think the next thing to add is indexes.  Currently everything works
via a linear scan of the database.

BY [Chris Morris](https://www.fastcompany.com/user/chris-r-morris)

ChatGPT and Dall-E might get the lion’s share of the spotlight, but Mira Murati may be the real star of OpenAI.

Murati is the chief technology officer at OpenAI, the startup behind ChatGPT—the most advanced AI chatbot ever to be deployed to a mass audience—and _Fast Company_’s Most Innovative Company of 2023.

The job got even more interesting near the end of 2023. On November 17, she was named interim CEO after [former CEO Sam Altman was pushed out of the company](https://www.fastcompany.com/90985360/sam-altman-out-at-openai-cto-mira-murati-to-take-over). She relinquished that role soon after to Emmett Shear, then, on Nov. 29, Altman returned (formally) as CEO—and one of the first things he did was praise Murati’s leadership of the company in his absence. (She is now back in her previous role as CTO.)

“Mira did an amazing job throughout all of this, serving the mission, the team, and the company selflessly throughout,” [he wrote](https://openai.com/blog/sam-altman-returns-as-ceo-openai-has-a-new-initial-board). “She is an incredible leader and OpenAI would not be OpenAI without her. Thank you.” (Murati, [via Twitter/X](https://twitter.com/miramurati/status/1730032754887430352), was just as effusive in her support for Altman.)

Earlier this year, _Fast Company_ senior writer Ainsley Harris visited OpenAI and learned how Murati and the company innovate ( [read the full story here](https://www.fastcompany.com/90850342/openai-mira-murati-chatgpt-dall-e-gpt-4)).

Born in Albania and a veteran of Tesla, where she led development of the Model X, Murati has been with OpenAI since 2018. While the world gawks at the technology and argues about its potential, she and her team are focused on improving OpenAI’s products. After all, billions of dollars are riding on the success of their technology, which could be Silicon Valley’s most disruptive since the iPhone.

The honing of the tech, as well as the global conversation around it, is likely to go on for quite a while. In the meantime, here are a few things to know about Murati.

## **She has a long history of influencing how people interact with new technology**

Murati’s interest in AI started in 2013, when she joined Tesla. At the time, the company was releasing early versions of Autopilot, its AI-enabled driver-assistance software, and working on AI-enabled robots for its factories. That led Murati to consider other real-world applications.

She moved over to Leap Motion in 2016 to work on the startup’s augmented reality system to replace keyboards. As VP of product and engineering, she hoped to make interacting with a computer “as intuitive as playing with a ball,” she tells _Fast Company’_ s Harris. But she soon realized the VR-based technology wasn’t close to being ready for a mass audience.

As Murati considered her next move, Harris writes, she came to the conclusion that a “massive advancement in technology” would have to play a role in solving the world’s biggest challenges. That led to her joining OpenAI in 2018, and her oversight of the rollouts of Dall-E and ChatGPT.

## **She’s one of the primary advocates of public testing**

Long before Google’s Bard rolled out or anyone had heard of Elon Musk’s Grok, OpenAI’s products were widely available.

Murati is passionate about testing these products with the general public. “You could make technological progress in a vacuum without real-world contacts,” she tells _Fast Company_. “But then the question is, Are you actually moving in the right direction?”

Building artificial general intelligence (AGI) solely within the confines of the lab, [she argues](https://fortune.com/longform/chatgpt-openai-sam-altman-microsoft/), can make the societal shock even more disturbing when it does come out. Earlier this year, my company acquired a Data & AI consultancy based in Pristina, Kosovo. That was my reason for visiting —to meet the people behind the team we were joining forces with.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
![]()
Townhall held in Pristina, Kosovo on September 15, 2025
During my trip earlier this week, I spent time with one of the founders DJ. His passion for Kosovo’s future was so strong it almost brought me to tears. Only afterward, when I looked at[DJ’s LinkedIn profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/driloni/), did I see the line that perfectly captured what I had just experienced:
*“On a mission to make Kosovo #1 in agentic AI builders per km² —more than anywhere else on Earth.”*
Those words weren’t marketing. They were the conviction of someone who believes deeply in his country and its people. And his declaration made me see Kosovo not just as a new partner, but as a place with world-changing potential.
## **Young, Gritty, and Building**
Over three days in Pristina, I met dozens of computer science undergraduates and early-career professionals. They were young, gritty, and determined to make their mark. They weren’t just studying theory; they were experimenting, collaborating, and putting their skills to work with a confidence well beyond their experience.
Being around them reminded me of what it feels like to stand on the edge of new possibilities —the beginning of careers, the start of a new industry, the building of a country itself. I left not just with new connections, but with the conviction that I want to keep coming back.

# Meet Mira Murati, the 36-year-old tech prodigy who shot to fame at OpenAI and now runs a startup that’s a poaching target for Mark Zuckerberg
![Dave Smith](https://fortune.com/img-assets/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Smith_Dave_bio.jpg?format=webp&w=1440&q=100)
By
[Dave Smith](https://fortune.com/author/dave-smith/)
Dave Smith
Editor, U.S. News
Down Arrow Button Icon
October 3, 2025, 6:05 AM ET
Add us on
![Mira Murati in a red dress in front of some trees](https://fortune.com/img-assets/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/GettyImages-2151935071-e1759430729242.jpg?format=webp&w=1440&q=100)
Mira Murati, founder of Thinking Machines Labs and former CTO at OpenAI, attends the 2024 Met Gala Celebrating "Sleeping Beauties: Reawakening Fashion" at The Metropolitan Museum of Art on May 6, 2024 in New York City.Cindy Ord / MG24—Getty Images for The Met Museum and Vogue
She may not have the same name recognition as other tech execs like Tim Cook, Bill Gates, or Mark Zuckerberg—not yet, anyway—but Mira Murati is one of the most-watched entrepreneurs in Silicon Valley. The former chief technology officer from OpenAI, who left to launch her own AI startup last year, just celebrated a major milestone: Her company, Thinking Machines Lab, just[launched its first product this week](https://thinkingmachines.ai/blog/announcing-tinker/), called Tinker. Rather than be another generative-AI chatbot like ChatGPT, Tinker is designed to help researchers and developers fine-tune AI models without the need to manage massive computing infrastructure. The launch represents the first commercial product from Thinking Machines, which raised a record-breaking $2 billion in seed funding at a $12 billion valuation.[](https://www.wired.com/story/thinking-machines-lab-first-product-fine-tune/)
Murati, the 36-year-old Albanian-American engineer-turned-executive, has emerged as a defining figure in the AI boom. Her journey from a mechanical engineering student to the chief technology officer who helped create ChatGPT exemplifies the rapid transformation of both AI technology and the careers of those building it. More recently, her[ability to resist Mark Zuckerberg’s aggressive recruitment](https://www.wired.com/story/mark-zuckerberg-ai-recruiting-spree-thinking-machines/)efforts—including reported billion-dollar offers to acquire her company and poach her talent—has solidified her reputation as a leader willing to chart her own course in an industry dominated by tech giants.[](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mira_Murati)
### From Albania to the world stage
Born on December 16, 1988, in Vlorë, Albania, during the final years of the country’s totalitarian regime, Murati’s early life was shaped by political upheaval and economic uncertainty. Her parents, both high school teachers[who taught literature](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/behind-the-tech/mira-murati-chief-technology-officer-openai), encouraged her academic pursuits, but Murati told Microsoft’s CTO Kevin Scott in 2023 that she had an “organic interest towards math and science,” where she excelled in Olympiads and competitions throughout her schooling.[](https://kitrum.com/blog/empowering-women-in-tech-the-inspiring-story-of-mira-murati-cto-of-openai/)
At 16, Murati won a scholarship from United World Colleges—a program that brings together students from over 80 countries to promote intercultural understanding and social responsibility—to study at[Pearson](https://fortune.com/company/pearson/)College on Vancouver Island in British Columbia. But a[](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/education/news/mira-murati-education-qualifications-how-uwc-roots-and-dartmouth-training-shaped-the-visionary-behind-chatgpt-and-sora/articleshow/123000391.cms)fter graduating from Pearson in 2005, Murati pursued an unusual academic path that would prove prescient for her later career. She[enrolled in a dual-degree program](https://www.srdsjournal.eu/academicprofiles/Ermira-Murati.html), completing a Bachelor of Arts in Mathematics from Colby College in 2011 and a Bachelor of Engineering in Mechanical Engineering from Dartmouth College’s Thayer School of Engineering in 2012. This combination of liberal arts and engineering disciplines provided her with both analytical thinking skills and technical expertise, which would prove handy in her later roles in Silicon Valley.[](https://www.theaireport.ai/articles/mira-murati-net-worth-2025-ex-openai-ctos-fortune)

# Greylock’s First Researcher In Residence
[**twitter](http://twitter.com/share?url=https://greylock.com/firm-news/greylocks-first-researcher-in-residence/)[**linkedin](http://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://greylock.com/firm-news/greylocks-first-researcher-in-residence/)[![icon email](https://greylock.com/wp-content/themes/greylock-new/images/svg/icon-email-dark.svg)email]()
## Greylock has a storied history of partnering with exceptional Entrepreneurs in Residence, resulting in the identification, inception, and growth of market-defining companies. For instance, Mustafa Suleyman started Inflection.AI, Stuart Bowers joined Apple as a leader in AI and Robotics, and D.J. Patil went on to lead data science for the White House.
For the first time, we are looking for a proficient AI researcher to join us in the newly created role of Researcher in Residence, on a part-time basis. We’ve crafted this role for someone who might be transitioning between research or industry roles, or in late stages of a graduate program, looking to thoughtfully explore their next step.
Your time with Greylock will be structured to afford you the flexibility to focus on uncovering your next venture, be it as a founder, a team member in a scaling company, or in academia. Greylock’s network, reach, and company understanding will be a powerful catalyst for your efforts. All the while, you will collaboratively work with Greylock’s investment team and a selection of our AI portfolio companies to accelerate the most compelling opportunities at the frontier of AI.
If you are an ambitious AI researcher who would like to explore spending time with us, please reach out to researcher@greylock.com. We are excited to collaborate on helping to build the intelligent future together.
See below for more details on the role.
## Additional details:
As a Researcher in Residence at Greylock, you will be at the nexus of pioneering research and practical application. Your collaboration with our investment team and Greylock-backed entrepreneurs will be instrumental in steering and propelling the next generation of AI-centric products.
This role offers the opportunity for an experienced AI researcher to deliberately explore their next move, while collaborating closely with us in our AI investing practice, and applying your expertise to help accelerate our portfolio of AI companies. We hope to partner with you for at least six months and are flexible to extend as our partnership progresses. This is a paid position.
## Our ideal candidate will possess:
* A robust technical foundation, ideally anchored with a Ph.D. in Computer Science or a pertinent field. Substantial industry experience in a research

[![View profile for Chloe Shih]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/chloe-shih?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Chloe Shih](https://www.linkedin.com/in/chloe-shih?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)Chloe Shih is an Influencer
5mo
* [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/posts/chloe-shih_microsoft-microsoftai-copilot-activity-7342973808506454016-FewJ&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)
Meet Mustafa Suleyman, CEO of[Microsoft AI](https://mx.linkedin.com/company/microsoft-ai?trk=public_post-text), previous co-founder of[Inflection AI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/inflectionai?trk=public_post-text)and[Google DeepMind](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/googledeepmind?trk=public_post-text), and my FIRST meeting at Cannes Lions 😳Last week was the first time that creators had a \*significant\* presence at[Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/cannes-lions-international-advertising-festival?trk=public_post-text), the world’s most important event for advertising, media, and marketing.[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text)asked if I’d be down to meet with Mustafa, and I was like “UH YES!”
This was our first time meeting, so I wanted to keep the topics lightweight and fun. It was also scorchingly hot on the beach. We were all melting with no AC 🥲 so please excuse me if I look like I’m about to faint.
The goal for this chat was to get to know Mustafa on a more human level and hear where his head's been at during his first time at Cannes Lions! Hope we’re able to extend these Tech Talks into fuller podcast episodes where we can really get into the nitty gritty. 🙂↕️🔥𝗥𝗮𝗽𝗶𝗱𝗙𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗧𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗰𝘀:
1. Highlights + Challenges of Mustafa’s first year as CEO of Microsoft AI
2. Goals and learnings at Cannes Lions
3. Prepared for networking here? 👀4. How do you recharge?
5. Book recommendations
6. Mustafa’s vision for Copilot
7. Where were you during AlphaGo? What did you think?
8. Mustafa’s superpower 👀9. Most embarrassing thing you’ve asked Copilot
10. The 4Qs of AI: IQ, EQ, AQ, SQ
11. AI’s impact on video creation
12. Advice for new grads 🎓13. Mustafa’s personal goal this year
Hope you enjoyed this casual tech talk on the beach with Mustafa! There is a chance we can do a more formal, longer podcast, so drop any of your questions you'd want me to ask him next time! 👇🏻\_\_\_
Produced by: Chloe Shih

**Short description**: Vietnamese-American computer scientist

**Quoc Viet Le** (born 1982), or **Quoc Le**, **Le Viet Quoc**, is a Vietnamese-American computer scientist and a machine learning pioneer at [Google Brain](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Company:Google_Brain), which he established with others from Google. He co-invented doc2vec[\[1\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-1) and [seq2seq](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Seq2seq)[\[2\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-2) models in [natural language processing](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Natural_language_processing). Le also initiated and lead the AutoML initiative at Google Brain, including the proposal of [neural architecture search](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Neural_architecture_search).[\[3\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-3)[\[4\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-tipsmake-4)[\[5\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-Wired-5)[\[6\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-6)

## Contents

- [1Education and career](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#Education_and_career)
- [2Honors and awards](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#Honors_and_awards)
- [3See also](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#See_also)
- [4References](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#References)

## Education and career

Le was born in Hương Thủy in the Thừa Thiên Huế province of Vietnam.[\[4\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-tipsmake-4) He studied at Quốc Học Huế High School.[\[7\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-7) In 2004, Le moved to Australia and attended Australian National University for Bachelor's program, during which he worked under Alex Smola on [Kernel method](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Kernel_method) in machine learning.[\[8\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-8) In 2007, Le moved to [Stanford University](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Organization:Stanford_University) for graduate studies in computer science, where his PhD advisor was [Andrew Ng](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Andrew_Ng).

In 2011, Le became a cofounder of Google Brain along with his then PhD advisor Andrew Ng and fellow student [Jeff Dean](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Jeff_Dean) and [Google](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Company:Google) researcher Greg Corrado.[\[4\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-tipsmake-4) In 2014, [Ilya Sutskever](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Ilya_Sutskever), [Oriol Vinyals](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Oriol_Vinyals) and Le proposed the [seq2seq](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Seq2seq) model for [machine translation](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Machine_translation). In the same year, [Tomáš Mikolov](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Tom%C3%A1%C5%A1_Mikolov) and Le proposed the doc2vec model for representation learning of documents.

## Honors and awards

Le was named MIT Technology Review's innovators under 35 in 2014.[\[9\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-9) He has been interviewed by and his research has been reported in major media outlets including Wired,[\[5\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-Wired-5) [_The New York Times_](https://www.wikipedia.org/wiki/The%20New%20York%20Times) ,[\[10\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-10) the Atlantic,[\[11\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#cite_note-11) and the MIT Technology Review.[\[12\]](https://handwiki.org/handwiki.org#c
